title: Faisceaux: Sheaf of Modules
snippet: In establishing what scheme is, we’ve had to use some of the language of sheaves applied to, specifically, the category of rings, where we have a sheaf of rings $\mathscr O _X$ on some topological space $X$. However, as in learning commutative algebra, we should also discuss what sheaves produce when talked about in the sense of modules—modules are essential in the study of rings; the category $\mathfrak{Mod} _A$ of $A$-modules is a fundamental invariant of a ring $A$. As motivation, we should note here, although possibly not having seen many (or any) examples, that not all important sheaves are sheaves of rings, and not all sheaves that can give us information about a scheme sit inside its structure sheaf. For example, the twisting sheaf $\mathscr O (1)$ of Serre on a projective scheme.1. Sheaves of ModulesDefinition 1.0.1. Let $X$ be a topological space, and let $\mathscr F$ and $\mathscr F ^{\prime}$ be two (pre)sheaves on $X$. We define a (pre)sheaf $\mathscr F \times \mathscr F ^{\prime}$ on $X$ by setting \[ (\mathscr F \times \mathscr F ^{\prime}) (U) := \mathscr F (U) \times \mathscr F ^{\prime}(U) \] where the restriction maps are the products of the restriction maps for $\mathscr F$ and $\mathscr F ^{\prime}$.Definition 1.0.2. Let $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ be a ringed space. An $\mathscr O _X$-module is a sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$ together with two morphisms (addition and scalar multiplication) of sheaves \[\begin{align}\mathscr F &\times \mathscr F \to \mathscr F, \; \; \; (s, s ^{\prime}) \mapsto s + s ^{\prime}\; \; \; \text{ for $s, s ^{\prime} \in \mathscr F(U), U \subseteq X$ open} \newline\mathscr O _X &\times \mathscr F \to \mathscr F, \; \; \; (a, s) \mapsto as \; \; \; \; \; \; \; \; \, \text{ for $a \in \mathscr O _X (U), s \in \mathscr F (U), U \subseteq X$ open}\end{align}\] such that addition and scalar multiplication by $\mathscr O _X (U)$ define on the group $\mathscr F (U)$ the structure of an $\mathscr O _X (U)$-module. Furthermore, for each inclusion $U\subseteq V$, the restriction homomorphism $\mathscr F(U) \to \mathscr F(V)$ is compaitble with the module structures via the ring homomorphism $\mathscr O _X (U) \to \mathscr O _X (V)$, which is to say the diagram commutes where the vertical arrows represent restriction maps and horizontal ones multiplication maps; alternatively, we can write that for any open $U \subseteq V$ in the topology of $X$, with $a, b \in \mathscr F (V)$ and $\lambda \in \mathscr O _X (V)$ \[ (a+b) \mid _U = a \mid _U + b \mid _U \; \; \text{and} \; \; (\lambda a) \mid _U = \lambda \mid _U a \mid _ U. \] Lastly, morphisms of $\mathscr O _X$-modules are simply maps $\varphi \colon \mathscr F \to \mathscr G$ between $\mathscr O _X$-modules considered as abelian sheaves that respect the multiplication by sections of $\mathscr O _X$. This is to say for an open $U \subseteq X$ the map $\varphi _U \colon \mathscr F (U) \to \mathscr G (U)$ is an $\mathscr O _X (U)$-module homomorphism, i.e. \[ \begin{align}\varphi _U (s + s ^{\prime}) &= \varphi _U (s) + \varphi _U (s ^{\prime}) \, \newline\varphi _U (as) &= a \varphi _U (s)\ \end{align} \] for all $s, s ^{\prime} \in \mathscr F(U)$ and $a \in \mathscr O _X (U)$.From the construction done above in the definition, we have obtained a category of $\mathscr O _X$ modules, which we denote by $\mathscr O _X \mathfrak {Mod}$, and it is well known that this category is indeed an abelian category. We should note here that the $\mathscr O _X$-module $\mathscr F$ such that $\mathscr F (U) = \{ 0 \}$ for all open sets $U \subseteq X$ is called the zero module and is simply denoted by $0$.Examples 1.0.3. Let $A$ be a ring. Let $X$ be a space that consists of a single point and $\mathscr O _X$ be the sheaf of rings with $\mathscr O _X (X) = A$. Then it immediately follows that an $\mathscr O _X$-module $\mathscr F$ is just an $A$-module $M$, where $M = \mathscr F(X)$ attached to $\mathscr F$. Let $X$ be a topological space and let $\underline{\zz} _X$ be the constant sheaf of rings on $X$ with value $\zz$. Then a $\underline{\zz} _X$-module is just a sheaf of abelian groups on $X$.

title: Algebraic Spaces & (Pre)Stacks
snippet: The theory of algebraic spaces and stacks arised from the study of moduli spaces in algebraic geometry. Loosely speaking, a moduli space is a space $\mathcal M$ where there is a $1:1$ correspondence between points of $\mathcal M$ and isomorphism classes of certain types of algebra-geometric objectcs. For example, the moduli space $\mathcal M$ could be a topological space, complex manifold, or an algebraic variety and the isomorphism classes of particular objects could be of smooth curves or vector bundles on a fixed curve. Moreover, moduli spaces come from the attempt to answer the classification problem; for example, in algebraic geometry, we would want to classify projecetive varieties, or we could want to classify all vector bundles on a fixed variety—the moduli space is itself the solution to the classification problem.1. Stacks1.1. Sites & SheavesThis section aims to describe the basic aspects of Gorthendieck topologies/sites. The idea of Grothendieck is that if we wanted to develop the tools of sheaves of topological spaces we wouldn’t need to rely strictly only to topological spaces. We can develop this machinery in a more general way of considering a category with certain collections of maps which are called coverings. A category that has such coverings is called a site. For example, if we had the category of open subsets of topological space (which what the machinery of sheaves is normally developed under the guise of), with morphisms being inclusions, is such an example of a site. In particular, in the algebraic geometry realm, we want to consider a topology on a scheme where étale morphisms are the open sets.Definition 1.1.1. A Grothendieck topology on a category $\mathcal S$ consists of the following data: for each object $X \in \mathcal S$, there is a set $\text{Cov} (X)$ consisting of coverings of $X$, that is, a collection of morphisms $\{ X_i \to X \} _{i \in I}$ in $\mathcal S$. We require the following properties: If $X ^\prime \to X$ is an isomorphism, then $(X ^\prime \to X) \in \text{Cov} (X)$. If $\{ X_i \to X \} _{i \in I} \in \text{Cov} (X)$ and $Y \to X$ is any morphism, then the fiber product $X_i \times _X Y$ exists in $\mathcal S$ and the collection \[ \{ X_i \times _X Y \to Y\} _{i \in I} \in \text{Cov}(Y). \] If $\{ X_i \to X \} _{i \in I} \in \text{Cov}(X)$ and $ \{ X _{ij} \to X _i \} _{j \in J _i} \in \text{Cov}(X_i)$ for each $i \in I$, then \[ \{ X _{ij} \to X_i \to X \} _{i \in I, j \in J_i} \in \text{Cov}(X). \]A site is a category $\mathcal S$ with a Grothendieck topology.Example 1.1.2. (Classical Topology) Let $X$ be a topological space, and let $\mathcal S$ be the category with objects as open subsets of $X$ and morphisms being inclusions; if $U \subseteq V$ then there is exactly one morphism from $U$ into $V$ and no other morphisms. For an object $U$ in $\mathcal S$, we define $\text{Cov}(U)$ to be the collections $\{ U_i \to U \}$ where $U = \bigcup _{i} U_i$. This defines a Grothendieck topology on $\mathcal S$. In particular, we can note that if $X$ is scheme, the Zariski-topology on $X$ produces a site, which is commonly reffered to as the small Zariski site.Example 1.1.3. (Small étale site). Let $X$ be a scheme. We define $X _{ét}$, which is called the small étale site on $X$, to be the full subcategory of $\mathfrak {Sch} /X$ whose objects are étale morphisms $U \to X$, i.e. consisting of schemes étale over $X$. A collection of morphisms $\{ U_i \to U \} _{i \in I}$ is in $\text{Cov} (U)$ if the map $\bigsqcup _i U_i \to U$ is surjective.Example 1.1.4. (Big étale site). The big étale site is the category $\mathfrak {Sch}$ where a covering of a scheme $X$ is a collection of étale morphisms $\{ U_i \to X \}$ in $\mathfrak {Sch}$ such that $\bigsqcup _i U_i \to X$ is sujective. We denote the big étale site by $\mathfrak{Sch} _{Ét}$.Example 1.1.5.(fppf site). Let $X$ be a scheme. We define $X _{\text{fppf}}$ to be the site where the underlying category is the category $\mathfrak{Aff}/X$ of affine schemes over $X$. We define a Grothendieck topology on this category by declaring that a collection of morphisms $\{ f \colon U _i \to Y \} _{i \in I}$ is a covering family if each $f_i$ is a flat morphism that is also of locally of finite presentation, and we have that $Y = \bigcup _i f_i (U_i)$.

title: Schemes I
snippet:
Classical algebraic geometry’s central focus is the study of algebraic varieties, where spaces are locally described as being loci of sets of polynomial equations over an algebraically closed field—much work is typically done over $\cc$. From the previous post, we can define an affine algebraic $k$-variety (or simply affine variety) as an irreducible Zariski-closed subset of $k^n$ for an algebraically closed field $k$, and a quasi-affine variety is an open subset of an affine algebraic variety. Although these definitions provide us with the foundations of classical algebraic geometry, they can in fact be generalized in the language of schemes. The theory of schemes is done in the context of the spectrum of a ring, say, $A$, and it has been noted that it was André Martineau who initially suggested to Serre to use $\spec A$ as a new way of doing algebraic geometry. Furthermore, in this context, an affine variety $V(f_1, \ldots f_r)$ of $k^n$ can be recovered from the affine scheme $\spec k [T_1, \ldots, T_n] / (f_1, \ldots, f_r)$.A great benefit of working over with schemes (although we haven’t defined them yet, just think of $\spec A$ with the Zariski topology) is that we’re working over something that need not be an algebraically closed field, which is something we need to with varieties. Additionally, the theory of schemes provides us with allowing nilpotents—something that’s not allowed in the classical setting. If we were to consider the equation $f(x) = x $ and $g(x) = x^2$, then $V(f) = V(g)$ as $x = 0$ and $x^2 = 0$ provide the same solution set. The variety here is blind to the multiplicity of the zeros, yet the variety will give rise to different schemes as we compare $k[x]/(x) \simeq k$ and $k[x] /(x^2)$—we will look at this particular example later on. For some generality, an affine $k$-variety is $\spec A$ for some finitely generated $k$-algebra $A$, but this can be further generalized: A $k$-variety is a scheme of finite type over the field $k$ (we’ll begin to look further into this later on). For a refresher on some algebra terminology see the Appendix of this post. Now, a great discussion was given by Eisenbud in his The Geometry of Schemes on pg. $8$. To paraphrase him: given a ring $A$, the affine scheme $\spec A$ “models and generalizes the relationships between an affine variety and its coordinate ring”. Additionally, classical algebraic geometry can be seen to be developed by looking at the bijective correspondenceAnd scheme theory is produced by dumping a lot of the assumptions on the right side of the correspondence, where the space on the right will simply become the space of rings. As a result, the object that will correspond to being the geometric objects of rings are affine schemes.1. Endowing $\spec A$ with a topology.Wwe define the Vanishing set, for any ideal $\mathfrak{a}$ of a ring $A$, to be the subset $V(\mathfrak{a}) \subseteq \spec A$ given by \[ V(\mathfrak{a}) := \{ \p \in \spec A \colon \mathfrak{a} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}. \} \] This is, equivalently, the set of points on which all elements of $\mathfrak{a}$ are zero, that is, $x \in V(\mathfrak a)$ if $x \in \spec A$ and $f(x) = 0$ for all $f \in \mathfrak {a}$. Now, understanding this last point where $f$ is a function of $\mathfrak{a}$ is established on the fact if $\mathfrak p \in \spec A$, then the localization $A _{\mathfrak p}$ is a local ring (see Lemma 1.1 below) with a maximal ideal $\mathfrak p A _{\mathfrak p} :=\mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak p}$. The reason being is that we can define a so-called residue field $\kappa (\mathfrak p):=A _{\mathfrak p}/ \mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak p}$ where a function/element $f \in \mathfrak{a}$ reduces modulo $\mathfrak p$ produces an element in $f (\mathfrak{p}) \in \kappa (\mathfrak p)$. On this last point, the value at the point $\mathfrak p$, which we will soon regard as just a point of $\spec A$, is just $f \Mod{ \mathfrak p}$. For example, the function $5 \in \mathfrak a = (5)$ of $ \spec \zz$ takes the value $1 \Mod{ 2}$ at the prime ideal $(2)$, $2 \Mod{3}$ at $(3)$, and $0 \Mod{5}$ at $(5)$—the point vanishes at the point $(5)$. Furthermore, an element $f$ of the ring lying in a prime ideal $\mathfrak p$ is equivalent to saying that the function vanishes at the point $\mathfrak p$; that is, $f(\mathfrak p) = 0$ if and only if $f \in \mathfrak p$.Lemma 1.1. Let $A$ be a ring and $\mathfrak{m}$ be a proper ideal of $A$. The following are equivalent: (1)$\; \;$ $A$ is a local ring with maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}$, (2)$\; \;$ $\spec A$ has exactly one closed point, (3)$\; \;$ $\mathfrak{m}$ is maximal and all elements in $1+\mathfrak{m}$ are invertible, and (4)$\; \;$ All elements in $A \setminus  \mathfrak{m}$ are invertible. Example 1.1.The localization of $A$ at some prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}$, $A _\mathfrak{p}$, is a local ring with a unique maximal ideal $\mathfrak{p} A _{\mathfrak{p}}$. The reason this is true is as follows: Consider the ideal $\mathfrak p A _\mathfrak{p}:=\mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak{p}}$. Then we have elements of the form $ rac{p}{1} \cdot  rac{a}{b} =  rac{ap}{b} \in \mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak{p}}$, where $p \in \mathfrak p$, $a \in A$, and $b \in A\setminus \mathfrak{p}$. Thus we have that $\mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak{p}} = {  rac{x}{y} \colon x \in \mathfrak p, y \in A \setminus \mathfrak p }$. Note that all elements in $\mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak{p}}$ are non-units. Now all elements in $A _{\mathfrak{p}} \setminus \mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak{p}}$ are of the form $ rac{s}{t}$ where $s 
otin \mathfrak p$ and $t \in A _p \setminus \mathfrak p$, Recall the fact that $ rac{a}{b} \in A _\mathfrak p $ is a unit if and only if $a \in A \setminus \mathfrak p$. So then we have that $ rac{s}{t}$ is a unit and thus by (4) in Lemma 1.1, we have that $A _\mathfrak p$ is a local ring with unique maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m} _{\mathfrak{p}}$. Now we want to proceed with endowing $\spec A$ with a topology, which we mostly do so in the following Lemma.Lemma 1.2. (a) If $\mathfrak a$ and $\mathfrak b$ are two ideals of $A$ then $V(\mathfrak {ab}) = V (\mathfrak{a}) \cup V( \mathfrak b)$. (b) If $\{ \mathfrak a _i \}$ is a family of ideals of $A$, then $V\left ( \sum \mathfrak a _i ight) =  igcap V( \mathfrak a _i)$. (c) If $\mathfrak{a}$ and $\mathfrak b$ are two ideals, then $V(\mathfrak a) \subseteq V (\mathfrak b)$ if and only if $\sqrt{\mathfrak b} \subseteq \sqrt{\mathfrak a}$.Proof. (a) Suppose $ \mathfrak{ab} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}$. If $\mathfrak{a} \subseteq \mathfrak p$, then we’re done, and so assume that $\mathfrak{a}$ is not contained in $\mathfrak p$. Then there is some $a \in \mathfrak a$ such that $a 
otin \mathfrak p$. But then for every $b \in \mathfrak b$, we have $ab \in \mathfrak{ab} \subseteq \mathfrak p$, and as $\mathfrak p$ is prime then $a \in \mathfrak p$ or $b \in \mathfrak p,$ so then $b \in \mathfrak p$. Thus $\mathfrak b \subseteq \mathfrak p$. So the forward direction of the equality holds. Now suppose $\mathfrak{q} \in V (\mathfrak a) \cup V (\mathfrak b$). WLOG, suppose $\mathfrak p \supseteq \mathfrak a$. Then we have that $x = \sum _{i, j}^n a_i b_j \in \mathfrak a$ with $a_i \in \mathfrak a$ and $b_j \in \mathfrak b$, as $\mathfrak a$ is an ideal. The elements of $\mathfrak{ab}$ are precisly of the form as $x$, so $\mathfrak {ab} \subseteq \mathfrak a$, which implies $\mathfrak {ab} \subseteq \mathfrak p$. Hence the equality holds. (b) A prime ideal $\p \supseteq \sum \mathfrak a _i$ for a family of ideals $\{ \mathfrak a _i\}$ if and only if each $\mathfrak a_i$ is contained in $\p$, since $\sum \mathfrak{a}_i$ is the smallest ideal containg all of the individual ideals $\mathfrak a _i$. (c) Recall that for any ideal $\mathfrak I$, $\sqrt{\mathfrak I}$ is the interesection of all prime ideals that contain $\mathfrak I$. So then $\sqrt{ \mathfrak {b}} \subseteq \sqrt{\mathfrak a}$ if and only if $ V( \mathfrak a) \subseteq V( \mathfrak b)$. ▨Furthermore, we can see clearly that $V(\varnothing) = \spec A$ and $V ((0)) = \spec A$. Now Lemma 1.2 shows that finite unions and abritrary insections of the sets $V(\mathfrak a)$ are once vanishing sets, and hence we define a topology on $\spec A$ by taking the vanishing subsets $V(\mathfrak a) \subseteq \spec A$ to be the closed subsets of our topology—this topology is called the Zariski topology.Proposition 1.1. The closed points of $\spec A$ correspond to $V(\mathfrak m) = \{ \mathfrak m\}$ where $\mathfrak m$ is a maximal ideal of $A$.Proof. As every maximal ideal is prime, then $\mathfrak m \in V(\mathfrak m)$ as, trivially, $\mathfrak m \subseteq \mathfrak m$. This is the only prime ideal that contains $\mathfrak m$ as, by definition, the only other ideal that contains $\mathfrak m$ is $A$, but $(1) = A$ is not a prime ideal, so $V(\mathfrak m) = \{ \mathfrak m \}$. Moreover, if $\p$ is a prime ideal that isn’t maximal, then $\{ \p \}$ cannot be closed as any closed set $V(I)$ that contains $\mathfrak p$ will also contain some maximal ideal that contains $\p$. ▨Proposition 1.2. If $\mathfrak a$ is an ideal, then $V(\mathfrak a) = V (\sqrt{\mathfrak a})$.Proof. Clearly $\mathfrak a \subseteq \sqrt{\mathfrak a}$, then $V(\sqrt{ \mathfrak a }) \subseteq V(\mathfrak a)$ as any prime ideal containing $\sqrt{\mathfrak a}$, then the prime ideal also contains $\mathfrak a$. Now let $\p \in V(\mathfrak a)$ and take $x \in \sqrt{\mathfrak a}$. Then $x^n \in \mathfrak a$ for some $n$, which implies that $x^n \in \p$. As $\p$ is prime, a typical induction argument gives you that $x \in \p$. Thus $\sqrt{\mathfrak a } \subseteq \p$ and $\p \in V(\sqrt{\mathfrak a})$. ▨Corollary 1.1. If $V(\mathfrak a ) = V(\mathfrak b)$, then $\sqrt{\mathfrak a} = \sqrt{\mathfrak b}$.Now, for any element $f \in A$, we denote $D(f)$ to be the open complement of $V((f))$, i.e. $D(f) = \{ \p \in \spec A \colon f 
otin \p \}$ which is to say that $D(f)$ is the locus that doesn’t vanish at the point $\p$. We call $D(f)$ a distinguished open subset of $\spec A$Lemma 1.3. (a) If $f \in A$, then $D(f) \sqcup V(f) = \spec A$. (b) If $f \in R$, then $D(f) = \varnothing$ if and only if $f$ is nilpotent. (c) If $f = u g$ for some unit $u \in A$, then $D(f) = D(g)$. (d) If $I$ is an ideal of $A$, and $\p$ is a prime ideal of $A$ with $p 
otin V(I)$, then there exists an $f \in A$ such that $\p \in D(f)$, and $D(f) \cap V(I) = \varnothing$. (e) If $f,g \in A$, then $D(fg) = D(f) \cap D(g).$ (f) If $f_i \in A$ for $i \in I$, then $ igcup_{i \in I} D(f_i)$ is the complement of $V( \{ f_i \} _{ i \in I} )$ in $\spec A$. (g) If $f \in A$ and $D(f) = \spec A$, then $f$ is a unit.Proof. See Lemma 10.17.2 of The Stacks Project (SP), where part (a) is (11) and (g) is (17) on SP. ▨Remark 1.1. As we endowed $\spec A$ with the Zariski topology, we will now distinguish between prime ideals of the set $\spec A$ and points of $\spec A$: for a prime ideal $\mathfrak p \in \spec A$, we denote $[\p]$ as the corresponding point of the topology.Proposition 1.3. (EGA I 1.1.10.) (i) When $f$ ranges over $A$, the set $D(f)$ forms a basis of the topology of $X$. (ii) For every $f \in A$, $D(f)$ is quasi-compact. In particular, $X = D(1)$ is quasi-compact.Proof. (i) Let $U \subseteq X$ be open. By definition, $U = X \setminus V(E)$ where $E$ is a subset of $A$, and $V(E) =  igcap _{f \in E} V(f)$. Thus $U =  igcup _{f \in E} D(f)$. (ii) Since sets of the form $D(f)$ form a basis of the topology on $X$, then it suffices to show that if $(f _i) _{i \in I}$ is a family of elements of $A$ such that $D(f) \subseteq  igcup _{i \in I} D(f_i)$, then there exists a finite subsets $J$ of $I$ such that $D(f) \subseteq  igcup _{i \in J} D(f_i)$. Now, let $\mathfrak a$ be the ideal of $A$ generated by the family of elements $f_i$; by hypothesis, we have that $V(f) \supseteq V(\mathfrak a)$, so $\sqrt{(f)} \subseteq \sqrt {\mathfrak a}$; since $f \in \sqrt{(f)}$, there is some $n \geq 0$ such that $f^n \in \mathfrak a$. This means that $f^n$ belongs to the ideal $\mathfrak b$ generated by the finite subfamily $(f _i) _{i \in J}$, and so we have $V(f) = V(f^n) \supseteq V(\mathfrak b ) =  igcap _{i \in J} V(f _i)$, which means that $D(f) \subseteq  igcup _{i \in J} D(f_i)$. ▨Proposition 1.4. For each $f \in A$, the open subset $D(f) \subseteq X$ is canonically identified with the prime spectrum $\spec A_f$.2. Attaching a sheaf $\mathscr O$ to $\spec A$.As we’ve endowed $\spec A$ with the Zariski topology, we will define a sheaf of rings $\mathscr O$ on $\spec A$. We do this by letting for each open $U \subseteq \spec A$: \[ \mathscr{O} _X (U) = \left \{ s \colon U 	o  igsqcup _{ \mathfrak p \in U} A _{ \mathfrak p} \colon 	ext{ satisfies $(\dagger)$ } ight \} \] where the condition $(\dagger)$ says that given $[\mathfrak p] \in U$ we have $s([\mathfrak p] ) \in A _{\mathfrak p}$ and that $s$ is locally a fraction, i.e. given $[\mathfrak p] \in U$ there is an open neighborhood $[\p] \in V \subseteq U$ and $a, f \in A$ such that for every point $[\mathfrak q] \in V$, $f 
otin \mathfrak q$, and $s([\mathfrak q]) =  rac{a}{f}$.There is an obvious parallel to the set of all regular functions on a quasi-affine variety, however, in that scenario we have the luxury of working over an algebraically closed field where a regular function mapped from a quasi-affine variety $Y$ of an affine variety $X$ to the base field $k$, but with working over a ring $A$, there is a chances that $s([\mathfrak p])$ wouldn’t be mapped into a consistent underlying space, so we have to consider function into the various local rings.Proposition 2.1.1. Let $A$ be a ring, and $(\spec, \mathscr O)$ its spectrum. (a) For any $\mathfrak p \in \spec A$, the stalk $\mathscr O _{\mathfrak p}$ of the sheaf $\mathscr O$ is isomorphic to the local ring $A _{ \p}$. (b) For any element $ f \in A$, the ring $\mathscr O (D(f)) \simeq A_f$. (c) In particular, $\Gamma (\spec A, \mathscr O) = \mathscr O (\spec A) \simeq A$.Proof. (a) We need to firstly define a map $\mathscr O _{\mathfrak p} 	o A _{\mathfrak p}$: take some $[(U, s)] \in \mathscr O _{\mathfrak p}$, which means that we have $s \in \mathscr O_X (U)$ and $\mathfrak p \in U$, and so $s \colon U 	o  igsqcup _{[\mathfrak q] \in U } A _{\mathfrak q}$. To get an element in $A _{ \mathfrak p}$ given $s$, its natural to consider the image of $\mathfrak p$ under $s$, i.e. $s(\mathfrak p) \in A _{\mathfrak p}$. Now defining $s \mapsto s( \mathfrak p)$ gives our desired map from $\mathscr O _{\mathfrak p}$ to $A _{\mathfrak p}$. To show that the map is well defined is easy. Suppose $[(U, s)]= [(V, s)]$ in $\mathscr O _{\mathfrak p}$, which is to say that the two germs agree at their restriction at a common open set containing the point $[\mathfrak p]$, so there is an open $W \subseteq U \cap V$ such that $s \mid _W = t \mid _V$. As $\mathfrak p \in W$, then $s (\mathfrak p) = t (\mathfrak p)$, and so the map $\mathscr O _{\mathfrak p} 	o A _{\mathfrak p}$ is well defined. Now suppose that we have $[(U, s)], [(V, t)] \in \mathscr O _{\mathfrak p}$ such that $s(\p) = t(\p)$. As $[\p] \in U$, for $s \colon U 	o  igsqcup _{\mathfrak q \in U} A _{\mathfrak q}$ there is some open neighborhood $ [\p] \in U _{ [\p ]} \subseteq U$ and $a, f \in A$ such that $ rac{a}{f}$, $f 
otin \p$, and for all $\mathfrak q \in U _{\p}$, we have $s (\mathfrak q) =  rac{a}{f}$. In a similar fashion, we have $t \colon V 	o  igsqcup _{\mathfrak q \in V} A _{\mathfrak q}$ with some open neighborhood $\mathfrak p \in V _{\p } \subseteq V$ and $b, g \in A$, $g 
otin \mathfrak q$, with $t(\mathfrak q) =  rac{b}{g}$ for all $\mathfrak q \in V _{\mathfrak p}$. So our inital assumption implies that $s(\p ) =  rac{a}{f} =  rac{b}{g} = t( \p)$. Let $\mathfrak q \in U _{\p} \cap V _{\p}$. Then $ s (\mathfrak q) =  rac{a}{f} =  rac{b}{g} = t(\mathfrak q)$, which means that $s \mid _{ U _{\p} \cap V _{\p}} = t \mid _{ U _{\p } \cap V _{\p}}$, so $[(U, s)] = [(V, t)]$. Thus $s \mapsto s(\p)$ is injective. Lastly, let $ rac{a}{f} \in A _{\mathfrak p}$ with $a \in A$ and $f \in A \setminus \p$. We want to pick an open set $U$ containg $\mathfrak p$ and $s \colon U 	o  igsqcup _{\mathfrak q \in U} A _{\mathfrak q}$ such that $s(\mathfrak p) =  rac{a}{f}$. Suppose $f 
otin \mathfrak q$, that is, $\mathfrak q \in D(f)$. Let $U = D(f)$ and consider $s \colon U 	o  igsqcup _{\mathfrak q \in U} A _{\mathfrak q}$ which sends $\mathfrak q$ to image of $ rac{a}{f}$ in $A _{\mathfrak q}$. Thus we have $s( \p )=  rac{a}{f} \in A _{\mathfrak q}$, and so the map is surjective. Hence we have an isomorphism $\mathscr O _{\mathfrak p} 	o A _{\mathfrak p}$, where $s \mapsto s(\mathfrak p)$. (b) Take $f \in A$. We want to define a map $A _f 	o \mathscr O (D(f))$ and produce a bijection. Firstly, we define the map $\psi \colon A_f 	o \mathscr O (D(f))$ by sending $ rac{a}{f^n}$ to the section $s \in \mathscr O (D(f))$ which assigns to each $\mathfrak p$ the image of $ rac{a}{f^n} \in A _{\p}$. For injectivity, suppose $\psi ( rac{a}{f^n}) = \psi ( rac{b}{f^m})$, then for every $\mathfrak p \in D(f)$, we have that $ rac{a}{f^n}$ and $ rac{b}{f^m}$ have the same image in $A _{\p}$. So then by definition, there is an $h \in A \setminus \p$ such that $h(af^m - bf^n) =0$ in $A$. Now let $\mathfrak A$ denote the annihilator of $f^m a - f^n b$. Then $h \in \mathfrak A$, but $h 
otin \p$, so we can clearly see that $\mathfrak A 
subseteq \p$. This translates to: for any $\p \in D(f)$ we have $V(\mathfrak A ) \cap D(f) = \varnothing$. Thus $f \in \sqrt{ \mathfrak A}$ as $V(\mathfrak A) \cap D (f) = \varnothing$ is equivalent to saying $V(\mathfrak A) \subseteq V(f)$, so $\sqrt{(f)} \subseteq \sqrt{\mathfrak A}$. So then we have $f\in \mathfrak A$ such that $f^k (f^m a -f^n) = 0$ so then $ rac{a}{f^n} =  rac{b}{f^m}$. Hence $\psi $ is injective. Now we have to show the map $\psi$ is surjective, which is long and more difficult. For now, I refer the rest of the proof to Hartshorne II.2.2.(b). (c) This is simply the special case of (b) where $f =1$, and $D(f) = \spec A$. ▨Definition 2.1.2. A ringed space is a pair $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ consisting of a topological space $X$ and a sheaf of rings $\mathscr O _X$ on $X$. A morphism of ringed space $(X, \mathscr O _X) 	o (Y, \mathscr O _Y)$ is a pair $(f, f ^{\sharp})$ of a continuous map $f \colon X 	o Y$ and a map $f ^{\sharp} \colon \mathscr O _Y 	o f _{\ast} \mathscr O _X$ of sheaves of rings on $Y$, where $(f _{\ast} \mathscr O_X):= \mathscr O _X (f^{-1} (V))$ for any open set $V \subseteq Y$, and the map $f ^{\sharp}$ is called the pullback. The ringed space $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ is a locally ringed space if for each point $P \in X$, the stalk $\mathscr O _{X, P}$ is a local ring. A morphism of locally ringed spaces is a morphism $(f, f ^{\sharp})$ of ringed spaces, such that for each point $P \in X$, the induced map of local rings $f ^{\sharp} _P \colon \mathscr O _{Y, f(P)} 	o \mathscr O _{X, P}$ is a local homomorphism of local rings. Recall that a local homomorphism is defined by: If $A$ and $B$ are local rings with maximal ideals $\mathfrak m _A$ and $\mathfrak m _B$ respectively, a homomorphism $\varphi \colon A 	o B$ is called a local homomorphism if $\varphi ^{-1} (\mathfrak m _B) = \mathfrak m _A$. An isomorphism of locally ringed spaces is a morphism with a two-sided inverse. Thus a morphism $(f, f ^{\sharp})$ is an isomorphism if and only if $f$ is a homeomorphism of the underlying topological spaces, and $f^ {\sharp}$ is an isomorphism of sheaves.Lemma 2.2.2. (a) If $A$ is a ring, then $(\spec A, \mathscr O)$ is a locally ringed space. (b) A map of rings $\varphi \colon A 	o B$, there is an induced map of locally ringed spaces \[ (f, f ^{\sharp} ) \colon (\spec B, \mathscr O _{\spec B} ) 	o (\spec A, \mathscr O _{\spec A})\] satisfying the two properties: (i) The map $f ^{\sharp} (D(f))$, which fits into the diagram is the natural localization of the map $\varphi$, i.e. it is given by mapping $ rac{a}{f} \mapsto  rac{\varphi(a)}{\varphi(f)^n}$. (ii) The map induced by $f ^{\sharp}$ on stalks at $\p \in \spec B$ and $\varphi ^{-1} (\p)$ is the localization $A _{\varphi ^{-1} (\p)} 	o B _{\p}$ of $\varphi$.Proof. Suppose $\varphi \colon A 	o B$ is a homomorphism of rings. We define, naturally, a map $f \colon \spec B 	o \spec A$ by $f(\p) = \varphi ^{-1} (\p)$ for any $\p \in \spec B$. Now let $\mathfrak a $ be an ideal of $A$, so then $f ^{-1} (V(\mathfrak a)) = V(\varphi (\mathfrak a))$, which means that $f$ is continuous. Now we want to associate to $\varphi$ a map of sheaves of rings $f ^{\sharp} \colon \mathscr O _{\spec A} 	o f _{\ast} \mathscr O _{\spec B}$. As we have a basis for the Zariski topology, it suffices to do this over distinguished open sets $D(f)$. Now we should note here that $f^{-1} (D(f)) = D(\varphi (f))$, since $f^{-1}(D(f)) = \{ \p \subseteq B \colon f 
otin \varphi ^{-1} (\p) \} = \{\p \subseteq B \colon \varphi (f) 
otin \p \} = D(\varphi (f))$. Thus we have $\Gamma (D(f), f _{\ast} \mathscr O _{\spec B}) = B _{\varphi (f)}$, and from Proposition 2.1.1.(a) we can identify $\Gamma (D(f), \mathscr O _{\spec A}) = A_f$. So then the original map $\varphi \colon A 	o B$ localizes to a map $A _f 	o B _{\varphi (f)}$ where $ rac{a}{f} \mapsto  rac{\varphi(a)}{\varphi(f)^n}$, and this what we define $f ^{\sharp}$ on sections over $D(f)$. Now we need to show that this map is indeed well defined—we need to check that it is compatible with the restriction maps in the basis of the topology of the topology. Suppose there is an inclusion $D(g) \subseteq D(f)$, then we can write $g^m = cf$, and the map $f ^{\sharp}$ will send $ rac{a}{f^n}$ to $ rac{ac^n}{g ^{nm}}$. Thus $\varphi (g)^m = \varphi (c) \varphi(f)$, which makes the following diagram commute and this what we desired. Furthermore, for (ii): for $ \p \in \spec B$, the preimage $ \varphi ^{-1} (\p) \in \spec A$, the map $f ^{\sharp}$ induces a map of stalks \[ f ^{\sharp} _{\p} \colon \mathscr O _{\spec A, \varphi ^{-1} (\p) } 	o \mathscr O _{\spec B, \p} \] which is just the localization $A _{\varphi ^{-1} (\p)} 	o B _{\p}$. This makes the preimage of maximal ideal of $A _{\varphi ^{-1} (\p)}$ equals the maximal ideal in $B _{\p}$, which makes $f ^{\sharp} _{\p}$ a map of local rings. Hence $(f, f ^{\sharp})$ is a morphism of locally ringed spaces. ▨Corollary 2.2.3. If $A$ and $B$ are rings, then any morphism of locally ringed spaces from $\spec B 	o \spec A$ is induced by a homomorphism of rings $\varphi \colon A 	o B$ as in Proposition 2.2.2.(b).Proof. Suppose we’re given a morphism of locally ringed spaces $(f, f ^{\sharp})$ from $\spec B$ to $\spec A$. Taking global sections, $f ^{\sharp}$ induces a homomorphism of rings $\varphi \colon \Gamma (\spec A, \mathscr O _{\spec A}) = A 	o \Gamma (\spec B, \mathscr O _{\spec B}) = B$. For any $\p \in \spec B$, we have an induced local homomorphism by Lemma 2.2.2.(b.ii) $A _{\varphi ^{-1} (\p)} 	o B _{\p}$. ▨3. SchemesNow we proceed to actually define what object we have an interest in, which are, in a way, affine varieties for commutative rings—ref. Varieties as Schemes by Mark Haiman, where the main theorem established is the following:Theorem. Let $k$ be an algebraically closed field. The category of reduced algebraic $k$-schemes is equivalent to the category of classical algebraic varieties over $k$Definition 3.0.1. An affine scheme is a locally ringed space $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ which is isomorphic (as a locally ringed space) to the spectrum of some ring. A scheme is a locally ringed space $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ that has an open cover by affine schemes. We call $X$ the underlying topological space of the scheme $(X, \mathscr O _X)$, and $\mathscr O _X$ its structure sheaf. Typically we write just $X$ to denote the scheme $(X, \mathscr O _X)$. A morphism of schemes is a morphism of locally ringed spaces. An isomorphism is a morphism with a two-sided inverse.Remark 3.0.2. In some literature, one says that a scheme $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ is a locally ringed space in which every point of $X$ has an open neighborhood $U$ such that the topological space $U$, together with the restricted sheaf $\mathscr O _{X \mid _U }$ is an affine scheme. The restriction sheaf is given in a natural way: if $U$ is open in $X$, then we define a restricted sheaf $\mathscr O _{X \mid _U}$, where for every open $V \subseteq U$, we set $\mathscr O _{X \mid _U}(V) = \mathscr O _{X} (V)$.Proposition 3.0.3. If $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ is a scheme, then its affine open subsets form a basis for the topology of $X$.Proof. If $V$ is an open neighborhood for some $x \in X$, then by hypothesis there exists an open neighborhood $ W$ of $x$ such that $(W, \mathscr O _{X \mid W})$ is an affine scheme; let $A$ be its corresponding ring. In the space $W$, $V \cap W$ is clearly an open neighborhood of $x$; so there exists $f \in A$ such that $x \in D(f)$ is an open neighborhood of $x$ and $D(f)$ is contained in $W \cap V$ (Propoisition 1.0.3 (ii)). The ringed space $(D(f), \mathscr O _{X \mid D(f)})$ is then an affine scheme isomorphic to $A_f$, by Proposition 1.0.4. ▨Corollary 3.0.4. If $(X, \mathscr O _X)$ is a scheme, for every open subset $U$ of $X$, the ringed space $(U, \mathscr O _{X \mid U})$ is a scheme.We should continue our discussion of schemes with incorporating defining another class of schemes that’s extremely relevant and prominent in algebraic geometry: projective schemes. Working with projective schemes requires us to take a different approach as done with $\spec B$ for some ring $B$.Let $A = k[x_0, \ldots , x_n]$. If we take $f \in A$ and $P = (a_0 \colon \cdots \colon a_n) \in \pp^n$, then $f(P)$ doesn’t make sense. For example, $f(x_0, x_1) =x^2_1 - x_0$ then $f(1, 1) =0$ but $f(-1, -1) 
eq 0$, even though $(1 \colon 1)$ and $(-1 \colon -1)$ are the same point in $\pp^n$. We need to adapt our approach, and we do this by requiring a polynomial $f \in A$ of degree $d$ to be homogeneous, that is, \(f(\lambda x_0, \ldots, \lambda x_n) = \lambda^d f(x_0, \ldots, x_n) 	ext{ for all $\lambda \in k^\ast$},\) which implies that $(\lambda x_0, \ldots, \lambda x_n) = 0 $ if and only if $f(x_0, \ldots, x_n) =0$, and so this makes sense when $f(P) = 0$ or $f(P) 
eq 0$. So then if we let $T \subseteq A$ be a set of homogeneous polynomials, we define \(V(T) = \{ P \in \pp^n \colon f(P) = 0 	ext{ for all $f \in T$} \}.\) Moreover, for a homogeneous polynomial $f \in A$, we define $D(f) := \pp^n \setminus V(f)$, and as we should expect, the definitions on $V$ give rise to the Zariski topology on $\pp^n$. We say that a projective algebraic variety is a subset of $\pp^n$ of the form $V(T)$, together with the induced Zariski topology. To be clear, we have the following Zariski topology on (algebraic) projective varieties:Lemma 3.0.5. Projective Varieties satisfy the folowing axioms of the closed sets of a topology on $\pp^n_k$; that is, we have the following: The union of finitely many projective varieties is a projective variety. The intersection of any number of projective varieties is a projective variety, and The empty set and $\pp^n_k$ are projective varieties. Definition 3.0.6. A graded ring is a ring $S$ together with a decomposition into abelian groups \[ S =  igoplus_{d\geq 0} S_d \] such that for all $d 
eq e$, we have $S_d \cap S_e = { 0 },$ and $S_d \cdot S_e \subset S_{d+e}$. The elements of $S_d$ are called the homogeneous elements of degree $d$.An important example that we’ll use is that we can define \[ k^d [x_0, \ldots, x_n] := \{ f \in k[x_0, \ldots, x_n] \colon f 	ext{ is homogeneous of degree $d$} \} \cup \{0 \} \] to grade the ring $k[x_0, \ldots, x_n]$ by $k[x_0, \ldots, x_n] =  igoplus_{d \geq 0} k^d [x_0, \ldots, x_n ]$; an element $f \in S_d$ is homogeneous of degree d. Furthermore, an ideal $J$ in $S$ is called homogeneous if it is generated by homogeneous elements; that is, $J =  igoplus_{d \geq 0} (J \cap S_d).$ So an ideal $J$ is homogeneous if and only if every element $f \in J$ has a unique composition $f = f_0 +\cdots +f_N,$ where each $f_i \in J$ is a homogeneous of degree $d_i$. And a homogeneous ideal $I$ is called prime if and only if for any pair of homgeneous elemements $f, g$ in a graded ring $S$, we have $fg \in I$ if and only $f \in I$ or $g \in I$.  For a graded ring $S$, the irrelevant ideal of $S$ is $S_{+} =  igoplus_{d >0} S_d,$ and we define the set $\proj S$ to be the set of all homogeneous prime ideals $\mathfrak p$, which do not contain all of $S _{+}$. Now for a homogeneous ideal $\mathfrak a$ is a homogeneous ideal of $S$, we define the subset $V( \mathfrak a ) = \{ \p \in \proj S \colon \p \supseteq \mathfrak a \}$.Lemma 3.0.7. (a) If $\mathfrak a$ and $\mathfrak b$ are homogeneous ideals in $S$, then $V(\mathfrak{ab}) = V ( \mathfrak a ) \cup V (\mathfrak b)$.(b) If $\{ \mathfrak a _i \}$ is any family of homogeneous ideals of $S$, then $V \left( \sum \mathfrak a _i ight) =  igcap V(\mathfrak a _i)$.3.1. Categorical Relations between $\mathfrak{AffSch}$ and $\mathfrak{Rings}$.Affine schemes form a category $\mathfrak{AffSch}$, which is a subcategory of the category of locally ringed spaces. The work we did for Lemma 2.2.2. gave us a hint of the similarity between rings and affine schemes: the assignment $A \mapsto \spec A$ gives us a contravariant functor from the category $\mathfrak{Rings}$ to the category $\mathfrak{AffSch}$. Similarily, Corollary 2.2.3. gave us the other contravariant functor in the opposite direction: taking the global sections of the structure sheaf $\mathscr O _{\spec A}$ gives us the ring $A$ back. Moreover a map of affine schemes $f \colon \spec B 	o \spec A$ is associated with a map of sheaves $f ^{\sharp} \colon \mathscr O _{\spec A} 	o f _{\ast} \mathscr O _{\spec B}$. After taking global sections, \[A = \Gamma (\spec A, \mathscr O _{\spec A}) 	o \Gamma (\spec A, f _{\ast} \mathscr O _{\spec B}) = B .\] Now we naturally define a canonical map \[ \Gamma \colon \Hom _{\mathfrak{AffSch}} (X, Y) 	o \Hom _{\mathfrak{Rings}} (\mathscr O _Y (Y), \mathscr O _X (X)) \] where $(f, f ^{\sharp})$ is sent to the map $f ^{\sharp} (Y)\colon \mathscr O _Y (Y) 	o \mathscr O _X (X)$.Proposition 3.2.1. If $X$ and $Y$ are affine schemes, then the map $\Gamma$ is bijective.Proof. Let $X = \spec B$ and $ Y = \spec A$, which means $\mathscr O _{Y} (Y) = A$ and $\mathscr O _{X} (X) = B$. Recall that if $\varphi \colon A 	o B$ is a ring homomorphism, then we define $\spec (\varphi) \colon \spec B 	o \spec A$ where $\p \mapsto \varphi ^{-1} (\p)$. Now from Lemma 2.2.2.(b.ii) it follows that $\Gamma (\spec \varphi ) = \varphi$. So then it suffices to show that $\spec (\Gamma (f)) = f$ for a given morphism $f \colon X 	o Y$. Now let $\varphi = \Gamma (f)=f ^{\sharp} (Y) \colon A 	o B$. Take $x \in X$, which corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak q$ of $B$, and let $\mathfrak p$ of $A$ be the prime ideal corresponding to $f(x) \in Y$. Then the diagram commutes. Thus we have $\varphi (A\setminus \p)\subseteq B \setminus \mathfrak q$, so $\varphi ^{-1} (\mathfrak q)=\p$. Therefore $\spec \varphi$ induces the same map as $f$ on the underlying topological spaces. Additionally, we have two morphisms of sheaves $\mathscr O _Y 	o f _{\ast} \mathscr O _X$, one induced by $f$ and the other induced by $\spec \varphi$. Lastly, for all $x$, the induced map on stalks $f ^{\sharp} _x$ and $(\spec \varphi ) ^{\sharp}$ coincide with the map $A _{\p} 	o B _{\mathfrak q}$ above. Hence $f ^{\sharp} = (\spec \varphi) ^{\sharp}$. ▨Theorem 3.2.2. The two functors $\spec $ and $\Gamma$ are mutually inverse and define an equivalence between the categories $\mathfrak {Rings}$ and $\mathfrak{AffSch}$, where \[  egin{align}A & \longmapsto \spec A 
ewline\Gamma (X, \mathscr O _X) & \longleftarrow{aise{.4pt}{\hspace{-5pt}\shortmid}}\, X \end{align} \] From this discussion, we see that affine schemes $X$ are completely determined by their corresponding rings of global sections $\Gamma (X, \mathscr O _X)$, and morphisms between affine schemes $X 	o Y$ are in $1 \colon 1$ correspondence with ring homomorphisms $\Gamma (Y, \mathscr O _Y) 	o \Gamma (X, \mathscr O _X)$. This establishes that a map $f$ between two affine schemes is an isomorphism if and only if the corresponding map of rings $f ^ {\sharp}$ is an isomorphism.We should note here however that there is a stronger statement of Theorem 3.2.2 where we consider schemes in general rather than affine schemes:Theorem 3.2.3. Let $X$ be an a scheme, and let $A$ be a ring. The canonical map \[ \Hom _{\mathfrak{Sch}} (X, \spec A) 	o \Hom _{\mathfrak{Rings}} (A, \Gamma (X, \mathscr O _X)) \]given $(f, f ^{\sharp}) \mapsto f ^{\sharp} (X)$ is bijective.3.2. Relative SchemesWe want to establish a notion of relative schemes where we choose some base scheme $S$ to work with. We say that a scheme over $S$ is a scheme $S$ together with a morphism $f \colon X 	o S$, which we call the structure map or structure morphism. If we have two schemes over $S$, say, $X$ and $Y$, then a map between $X$ and $Y$ is a map $X 	o Y$ compatible with the two structure maps, i.e. the following diagram commutes Remark 3.2.1. The schemes over $S$ form a category $\mathfrak{Sch} /S$, and the set of morphisms as described above is denoted $\Hom _{S} (X, Y)$. To reiterate, the schemes over $S$ are morphisms of schemes $f \colon X 	o S$, also called relative schemes over $S$ or $S$-schemes*. A morphism $h \colon (f \colon X 	o S) 	o (g \colon Y 	o S)$ is a morphism $h \colon X 	o Y$ of schemes such that $g \circ h = f$. Remark 3.2.2. Ever scheme $Y$ is a scheme over $\spec \zz$. We can write $ Y =  igcup _i U_i$ where $U_i \simeq \spec A_i$ for ring $A_i$. Then as $\zz$ is the inital object in the category of rings, then there is a canonical homomorphism $\varphi _i \colon \zz 	o A_i$ which induces maps $\spec A_i 	o \spec \zz$. Composing these maps with the established isomorphisms $U_i \simeq \spec A_i$, we get a map $Y =  igcup U_i 	o \spec \zz$. On the level of categories, this says that $\spec \zz$ is the terminal object in $\mathfrak{Sch}$, i.e. every scheme is a $\zz$-scheme, and furthermore that $\mathfrak{Sch} = \mathfrak{Sch} / \zz$.Recall that if $B$ is an algebra over a ring $A$, then we say that $B$ is finitely presented if it is the quotient of a polynomial ring $A[x_1, \ldots, x_n]$ over $A$ by a finitely generated ideal. If $A$ is noetherian, every finitely generated algebra is finitely presented. Moreover, if $B$ is finitely presented over $A$ whenever we write $B = A[x_1, \ldots, x_n] / I$, the ideal $I$ is always finitely generated in $A[x_1, \ldots, x_n]$. Now, an algebra $B$ over $A$ is called finite if $B$ is a finitely generated $A$-module, which is to say that there elements $b_1, \ldots, b_n \in B$ such that $B = Ab_1 + \cdots + A b_n$. An algebra $B$ over $A$ is said to be finitely generated (or of finite type) if $B$ is a finitely generated $A$-algebra, i.e. there are elements $b_1, \ldots, b_n$ such that $B = A [b_1, \ldots, b_n]$. We can remark here that an algebra $A 	o B$ is finite if and only if it is of finite type and integral (e.g. $\zz 	o \zz [\sqrt{2}]$ is of finite type over $\zz$ and integral, so indeed finite).Definition 3.2.3. Let $X/S$ be a scheme over $S$ with structure morphism $f \colon X 	o S$. One says thati) $X/S$ is of locally finite type if $S$ has a cover consisting of open affine subsets $V_i = \spec A_i$ such that each $f ^{-1} (V_i)$ can be covered by affine subsets of the form $\spec B _{ij}$, where each $B _{ij}$ is finitely generated as an $A_i$-algebra; ii) $f$ is of finite type if, in (i), one can do with a finite number of $\spec B _{ij}$.In the special case where $S$ is affine, say, $S = \spec T$, one says that a scheme $X$ over $S$ is of locally finite type (resp. finite type) over $S$ if the structure morphism $X 	o \spec T$ is locally of finite type (resp. finite type). Speaking of special cases, our notion of a scheme being of locally finite type/of finite type is a weaker restriction of a stronger finiteness property a morphism can have:Definition 3.2.4. Let $f \colon X 	o S$ be a scheme over $S$. We say that i) $f$ is affine if there is a covering $\spec A_i$ of $S$ such that each $f ^{-1} (\spec A_i)$ is itself affine; ii) $f$ is finite if it is affine, and in the notation above, if for each $f ^{-1} (\spec A_i) = \spec B_i$, the $A_i$-algebra $B_i$ is a finitely generated $A_i$ module.Proposition 3.2.5. Let $X/S$ be a scheme over $S$ with structure morphism $f \colon X 	o S$. Then $X/S$ is of locally finite type (resp. finite type / resp. affine / resp. finite) if $X/S$ is of locally finite type (resp. finite type / resp. affine/ resp. finite) for one affine covering of $S$.3.3. Properties of SchemesWe seek to talk more about some of the internal structural properties of schemes throughout this section, establishing some of the necessary, and expected, terminology (e.g. open and closed subschemes). This section follows Hartshorne’s II.3 very closely.Definition 3.3.1. A scheme is connected if its topological space is connected. A scheme is irreducible if its topological space is irreducible.Definition 3.3.2. A scheme $X$ is reduced if for every open set $U$, the ring $\mathscr O _X (U)$ has no nilpotent elements. Equivalently, we can show that $X$ is reduced if and only if the local rings $\mathscr O _P$, for all $P \in X$, have no nilpotent elements.Definiton 3.3.3. A scheme $X$ is integral if for every open set $U \subseteq X$, the ring $\mathscr O _X (U)$ is an integral domain.Example 3.3.4. Let $X$ be an affine scheme with a corresponding ring $A$. Then $X$ is irreducible if and only the nilradical $	ext{nil} (A)$ of the ring $A$ is a prime ideal. Moreover $X$ is reduced if and only if $	ext{nil} (A) = 0$. Lastly, $X$ is integral if and only if $A$ is an integral domain.Proposition 3.3.5. A scheme is integral if and only if it is both reduced and irreducible.Proof. Suppose that the scheme $X$ is integral, then obviously as $\mathscr O _X (U)$, for open $U \subseteq X$, is an integral domain then it contains no nilpotents. Now suppose that the underlying space of $X$ is reducible, then we can find two nonempty disjoint open subsets $U _1$ and $U _2$ with $X = U_1 \cup U_2$. Then $\mathscr O _X (X) = \mathscr O _X (U_1 \cup U_2) \simeq \mathscr O _X (U_1) 	imes \mathscr O _X (U_2)$, where $s \mapsto (s \mid _U, s \mid _ V)$ for $s \in \mathscr O _X (X)$. The ring $\mathscr O _X (U_1) 	imes \mathscr O_X (U_2)$ has zero divisors of the form $(0, s)$ and $(s, 0)$ unless if either $U$ or $V$ is empty. This is a contradiction as the scheme $X$ is integral. For the opposite direction, suppose that the scheme $X$ is both reduced and irreducible.Definition 3.3.6. A scheme $X$ is said to be locally noetherian if it can be covered by open affine subsets $\spec A _i$, where each $A_i$ is a noetherian ring. The scheme $X$ is noetherian if it is locally noetherian and quasi-compact. Equivalently, $X$ is noetherian if it can be covered by a finite number of open affine subsets $\spec A _i$, where each $A_i$ is a noetherian ring.Proposition 3.3.7. A scheme $X$ is locally noetherian if and only if for every open affine subset $ U = \spec A$, the ring $A$ is noetherian. In particular, an affine scheme $X = \spec A$ is a noetherian scheme if and only if the ring $A$ is noetherian.Example 3.3.8. If $P$ is a point of some variety $X$, with the local ring $\mathscr O _P$, then we have that $\spec \mathscr O _P$ is an integral noetherian scheme.Definition 3.3.9. An open subscheme of a scheme $X$ is a scheme $U$, where $U \subseteq X$ is open, and whose structure sheaf $\mathscr O _U$ is isomorphic to the restriction $\mathscr O _X \mid _U$ of the structure sheaf of $X$. An open immersion is a morphism $f \colon X 	o Y$ which induces a isomorphism of $X$ with an open subscheme of $Y$.Definition 3.3.10. A closed immersion is a morphism $f\colon Y 	o X$ of schemes such that $f$ induces a homeomorphism of $\mid Y \mid$ onto a closed subset of $\mid X \mid$, and furthermore the induced map $f ^{\sharp} \colon \mathscr O _X 	o f _{\ast} \mathscr O _Y$ of sheaves on X is surjective. A closed subscheme of a scheme X is an equivalence class of closed immersions, where we say $f \colon Y 	o X$ and $f ^{\prime} \colon Y ^{\prime} 	o X$ are equivalent if there is an isomorphism $i \colon Y’ 	o Y$ such that $f ^{\prime} = f \circ i$.Refrences. EGA Hartshorne’s Algebraic Geometry

If someone has had an interest in pure mathematics for some time, then it’s almost inevitable to have heard about elliptic curves at some point. My first time hearing about elliptic curves, which we will define concretely in a bit, was when I was in high school when, I think, I watched a video relating to Fermat’s Last Theorem (FLT) and had heard that they were central to the proof FLT. For the purposes of this quick discussion, an elliptic curve is an equation of the form $y^2 = x^3 + ax^2 +bx+c$, where $a, b, c \in \zz$ and $x^3+ax^2 + bx+c$ doesn’t have the same root more than once. As a nice beginning discussion, suppose we wanted to answer the following question: Are there any consectutive integers whose product is a perfect square?We can write down the following which expresses the question in algebraic terms: $y^2 = x(x+1)(x+2)$, which expanded is $y^2 = x^3 + 3x^2 +2x$, so we have an elliptic curve, as per our definition. Now we can take $x=0$ and say that this answers our question, but this is uninteresting, so we should amend the criterion to our question that we are seeking a nontrivial solution, so we can reformulate our question to say something about counting: Does the elliptic curve $y^2 = x(x+1)(x+2)$ have solutions where $y 
eq 0$ and $x, y \in \zz$?One thing that can be noted quickly enough is that we’ll never have $y$ be an odd integer as $x^3+3x^2+2x$ can be inspected to always have an even output. We will come back to this in a bit, but I hope to approach other aspects of elliptic curves beforehand.In the past century, the connection between an elliptic curve and FLT was the spark that led to the proof of FLT. This spark was done by Gerhard Frey and Ken Ribet: Frey had conjectured that if such a triple $x, y, z$ such that $xyz 
eq 0$ satisfied FLT, then the elliptic curve \[E : y^2 = x(x-a^p)(x+b^p), 	ext{ where $p>2$ is prime},\]would contradict the Taniyama-Shimura-Weil (TSW) conjecture (meaning that $E$ is not modular). And it was Ribet who proved Frey’s conjecture. Eventually, as the story goes, Andrew Wiles (and Taylor Wiles) proved TSW for the special case of semistable elliptic curves. This covered the case of $E$ which implied $E$ cannot exist so no $x, y, z$ such that $xyz 
eq 0$ does not exist either. Theorem 1. (Taylor-Wiles) All semistable elliptic curves over $\qq$ are modular.Corollary 1. There does not exists a triple $x, y,z$ with $xyz 
eq 0$ such that $x^n+y^n = z^n$ for $n \geq 3$.Proof. The semistable elliptic curve $E : y^2 =x(x-a^p)(x+b^p)$ was shown by Ribet not to be modular, but Taylor-Wiles shows that gives us that all semistable curves are modular, and so we have a contradiction.Now we want to move on to a more “technical” discussion of elliptic curves. Definition 1. An elliptic curve over $\qq$ is a smooth cubic projective curve $E$ defined over $\qq$, with at least one rational point $\mathfrak{O} \in E( \qq)$ that we call the origin.We can boil this down: An elliptic curve is a curve in $E$ in the projective plane, meaning $\pp^2$, given by a cubic polynomial $F(X, Y, Z) = 0$ with rational coefficients, that is, egin{align} F(X, Y, Z) = \; & aX^3 +bX^2Y +cXY^2+dY^3 \ &+ eX^2 Z + fXYZ+gY^2 Z \ & + hXZ^2+jYZ^2+kZ^3 = 0 \end{align} with coefficients $a, b, c, \ldots \in \qq$ such that $E$ is smooth. Recall the fact $E$ is smooth means that $\left(  rac{\partial F}{\partial X} (P),  rac{\partial F}{\partial Y } (P),  rac{\partial F}{\partial Z} (P) ight)$, the tangent vector, does not vanish at any $P \in E$.Remark 1. There is often an abundance of people who will say that something is an elliptic curve in different ways other than Definition 1. The ones I often hear the most are:(1) An elliptic curve over a field $k$ is a smooth projective curve of genus $1$ defined over $k$ with a distinguished $k$-rational point. (2) ($k = \cc$) An elliptic curve over $\cc$ is a compact manifold of the form $\cc /L$ where $L = \zz + \omega \zz$ is lattice in the complex plane. I cannot speak to $2$ as this requires working knowledge of topological groups, which I’m almost completely unfamiliar with, however, I thought I’d just mention it. Now, this distinguished rational point over $k$ is an important detail that we should recall: If we let $\mathfrak{X}$ be a scheme over $k$, then $\ell$ is a rational point if $\kappa (\ell) \simeq k$, where $\kappa (\ell)$ denotes the residue field at the point $\ell$. ◄In the case where we’re taking coefficients from some field $F$, we write $E$ is defined over $E/F$ (e.g. as per our Definition 1., $ E / \qq$). Furthermore, even though we’ve defined an elliptic curve to be explicitly projective, we usually consider points of the form ${ (  X \colon Y \colon 1) }$, which are called affine charts of E. This means that we can study the affine curve  egin{align} &aX^3+bX^2Y+cXY^2+dY^3 \ &eX^2+fXY+gY^2+hX+jY+k = 0 \end{align} instead, but we keep in the back of our mind that this affine curve is omitting information about some points of $E$ at infinity (points of the form $( X \colon Y \colon 0)$ that satisfy $E$). Now, even though we have defined an elliptic curve to be as it is in Definition 1, it can be shown that an elliptic curve can be turned into a (general) Weierstrass equation; that is, an equation of the form \(y^2+a_1xy+a_3y=x^3+a_2x^2+a_4x+a_6\) where the base field we’re working is of any characteristic (see [Silv]). The fact that the base field is of any characteristic is a crucial point, since if we were working over a field $k$ with characteristic different of $2$ or $3$, then we can simply assume that we’re working with an elliptic curve $E/k$ defined by a short Weierstrass equation \(E: y^2 = x^3 +Ax+B\) in affine coordinates, or $zy^2 = x^3 +Axz^2+Bz^3$ in projective coordinates. For the rest of this discussion, we’re going to assume we’re working with fields of characteristic not $2$ or $3$. Now we should note here that $E: y^2 = x^3 + Ax+B$ is non-singular if and only if $4A^3+27B^2 
eq 0$ (this is an okay exercise about singularity). Additionally, the projective form of $E$ (we will denote it by $\widehat{E}$) has a unique point at infinity, the point $(0 \colon 1 \colon 0)$, which we also call the origin $\mathfrak{O}$ of $\widehat{E}$. In enough time, we will see that we can make $\widehat{E}$ into a group, where the point at infinity $\mathfrak{O}$ will play the role of the identity element. In fact, we’ll go about this right now.Let’s look at rational points on a curve $E \colon y^2 = x^3+Ax^2 + B$. We let $E( \qq ) = { (x, y) \in E \colon x, y \in \qq } \cup { \mathfrak{O} }$ where $\mathfrak{O} = [0 \colon 1 \colon 0]$ is the point at infinity. We generalize a process of adding points on an elliptic curve by using the example of $T : y^2 = x^3 -5x+8$.We start with two points $X$ and $Y$ on $T$ and draw a line through $X$ and $Y$. This line, say, $L$ meets a third point on $T$ at the point $K$. Now we draw a vertical line through $K$ that hits another point, $Z$, on the elliptic curve. We write $Z = P+Q$ and say that this is the sum of $P$ and $Q$ on $E$. Now, what if we want to add a point to itself, how do we go about doing this? We will take a line $L$ to be tangent to $T$ at a point $A$:This tangent line $L$ hits a point $B$, which we then reflect across the $x$-axis, and we call the resulting point $C$; this is the point $P+P := C$ or $2P := C$. Proceeding, as the group axioms require, what is the inverse of some point on $T$?We denote the reflected point of $P$ as $-P$, and this is our inverse. Now, as you can see, the vertical line through $P$ and $-P$ does not intersect at a third point on $T$. As hinted before, however, and in fact attached to our definition of $E (\qq)$, the third point on this line will be $\mathfrak{O}$, the point at infinity. This relates to a previous post where we associate a point at infinity with a line of slope $\infty$ (any vertical line $x= a$ in the affine plane corresponds to a projective equation $x = az$ and $(0 \colon 1 \colon 0 )$, so any vertical line passes through $\mathfrak{O}$). We can summarize this in the following remark.Remark 2. Let $E$ be an elliptic curve given by a Weierstrass equation $y^2=x^3+Ax+B$ with $A, B \in \qq$. Let $P$ and $Q$ be two rational points in $E (\qq)$ and we let $\mathfrak{L}$ be the line going through $P$ and $Q$, that is, $\mathfrak{L} = \overline{PQ}$. In the case that $P=Q$, then we let $\mathfrak{L}$ be the tangent to $E$ at $P$. As the curve $E$ is cubic, and we have that $\mathfrak{L}$ intersects $E$ at two rational points, then we will have that there is a third point of intersection $\mathfrak{L} \cap E$, which we will call $R$, that is also defined over $\qq$. \(\mathfrak{L} \cap E (\qq) = \{ P, Q, R \}\) We define $P +Q$ to be the reflection of the point $R$ over the $x$-axis. Now it’s quite simple to see that $P+Q = Q+P$. And we let $\mathfrak{O}$ be the zero element of $E (\qq)$, and for any given $P \in E(\qq)$ there exists a second point $-P$ such that $P+(-P) = \mathfrak{O}$. Now if we have that $E$ is given by $E: y^2 = x^3+Ax+B$ and $P = (x, y)$ then $-P = (x, -y)$. We should note here showing associativity is difficult and we will omit the proof here—you can find it online by a simple search. So $(E, +)$ is an abelian group. ◄An important thing to do when actually working with an elliptic curve as an abelian group, we need to know what the “output” of adding two points of $E$ actually is. We’re going to change some of the notation different from Remark $2$: Set \(P_1 (x_1, y_1), \; P_2 (x_2, y_2), \; P_1 \ast P_2 (x_3, y_3), \; P_1 + P_2 = (x_3, -y_3).\) You can compare this to our example of $E \colon y^2 =x^3-5x+8$, where $P_1 \ast P_2$ corresponds to the “third point”. The process to find $P+Q$ largely revolves around finding $(x_3, y_3)$, so we will go along assuming that we’re given $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. The line $\mathfrak{L}$ connecting $P_1$ and $P_2$ has the form $y = \lambda x + 
u$, where $\lambda = (y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$ and $
u = y_1-\lambda x_1 = y_2 - \lambda x_2$ (but note here that if $P_1 = P_2$, then $\lambda = (3x_1^2 + A)/2y_1)$. Now we can substitute $y^2 = (\lambda x + 
u)^2 x^3 + Ax+B$, so  egin{align}x^3+Ax+B-(\lambda x + 
u)^2 &= (x-x_1)(x-x_2)(x-x_3) \ &= x^3 - (x_1 + x_2 +x_3)x^2 + (x_1x_2+x_1x_3+x_2x_3)x-x_1x_2x_3, \end{align} so we can compare both sides. If we equate $x^2$ on both sides, this gives $-\lambda^2 = -x_1 -x_2 -x_3$, so $x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 -x_2$, and as a consequence we get $y_3$! We can compute $y_3$ using $y_3 = \lambda x_3 + 
u$. We can summarize our finding to easily work with $(E, +)$:Example 1. Consider $E \colon y^2 = x^3 + 17$. We can also see that $E$ is smooth as $4(0)^3+27(17)^2 
eq 0$. We can take the rational points $P_1 = (-1, 4)$ and $P_2 = (2, 5)$ on $E$ and find $P_1 + P_2$. We can find that $\lambda = 1/3$ and $
u = 13/3$, so $y = x/3 + 13/3$. So we can compute $P_1 + P_2 = (( rac{1}{3})^2 + 1 -2, ( rac{-1}{3})^3+1/3(1) -13/3) = (-8/9, -109/27)$. ◄Now we’d like to move on to talking about the structure of $E$ as an abelian group, in particular, for now, $E(\qq)$. Theorem 1. (Mordell-Weil) Let $E$ be an elliptic curve given by an equation $E \, \colon y^2 = x^3 + Ax+B$ with $A,B \in \qq$. Then group of rational points $E(\qq)$ is a finitely generated generated abelian group.Recall that this means that there is a finite set of points $P_1, \ldots, P_k \in E (\qq)$ so that every point $P \in E(\qq)$ can be written in $P = n_1 P_1 + n_2 P_2 + \cdots + n_k P_k$ for some $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k \in \zz$. Now as a result of Moderll-Weil and some group theory, we it follows that \(E (\qq) \simeq E(\qq)_{	ext{tor}} \oplus \zz^r ,\) where $E(\qq){	ext{tor}}$ is the set of points $P \in E (\qq)$ such that there exists an $n \in \zz{\geq 0 }$ such that $n P = \mathfrak{O}$, and $\zz^r$ is just $r$ copies of $Z$ for some integer $r \geq 0$ and $\zz^r$ is generated by $r$ points of $E( \qq)$ with infinite order (so $P \in E (\qq)$ such that $nP 
eq \mathfrak{O}$ for all $n \in \zz$ with $n 
eq 0$). This positive integer $r$ is called the rank of the elliptic curve $E/\qq$. The more elusive aspect of $E (\qq)$ is the value of its corresponding rank, as $E(\qq)_{	ext{tor}}$ is well understood:Theorem 2. (Mazur) Either \(E(\qq)_{	ext{tor}} \simeq \zz/ n \zz \; 	ext{ or } \; E \simeq (\qq)_{	ext{tor}} \zz /2 \zz \oplus \zz /2m \zz,\) where $n \in [[ 1, 12 ]] \setminus {11 }$ and $m \in [[ 1, 4 ]]$. For the rank of an elliptic curve $E (\qq)$, we would wish (as we do not) to know if there is a way to compute the corresponding rank $r$ of $E (\qq)$, and additionally we would wish to know the possible values of $r$—can they be any integer, or is there a certain subset of integers that classify the rank of all elliptic curves $E(\qq)$. On this last note, an open question is also to ask if there’s a certain bound on $r$—how big can this thing possibly get? There’s one, more or less, recent result which gives some insight into the rank:Theorem 3. (Bhargava, Shankar 2010-2012) The average rank of all elliptic curves over $\qq$ is less than 1. References:[Silv] Joseph H. Silverman, The arithmetic of elliptic curves, 2nd ed., Springer, 2009.

title: Projective Spaces
snippet:
If we’re given two curves, and we wanted to study all the points of intersection of the given curves, then there is a possibility that these curves do not intersect. Namely, when the curves are parallel to one another. The idea behind projective geometry is that we want to enlarge the underlying plane we’re working with (an affine space) to have an intersection “at infinity” where the curves intersect, given that they didn’t already intersect in the original plane (this little stipulation is important). We want there to be at least one intersection between two curves, always. A way to attach these points at infinity is to embed the affine space $\aff^n$ into the vector space $k^{n+1}$ by identifying to each point $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \in \aff^n$ with the new  point $( 1, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \in k^{n+1}$; that is, we’re embedding $\aff^n 	o k^{n+1}$ by the map $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \longmapsto ( 1, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and then considering the $1$-dimensional linear subspace in $k^{n+1}$ spanned by this vector. Note that every point $(1 , x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ determines a line in $k^{n+1}$ through the origin of $k^{n+1}$. The fact that we’re working over a vector space here is important as this is a valid thing to do since if $v$ is a vector, we can define a line as the set of all  $\lambda v$ for all $\lambda \in k$, and the line passes through the origin as $\lambda = 0 \in k$. Additionally, every line through the origin of $k^{n+1}$, except for those lying in the plane $x_0 = 0$, corresponds to a unique point of the form $(1,  x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$. And the lines through the origin of $k^{n+1}$, in the plane $x_0 = 0$, can be thought of as corresponding to the points at infinity. As an example, if we take a point $(c_1, c_2) \in \aff^2$, this produces a point $(1, c_1, c_2) \in k^3$ and a corresponding line through the origin $(0, 0, 0)$.We define the projective plane, which is denoted $\pp^1 (\aff^2) = \pp (\aff^2)$, as the set of all such $1$-dimensional linear subspaces of $k^3$. This is equivalent to saying that the projective plane is the collection of all lines through the origin coming from points of $\aff^2$, with the addition of including lines contained in the plane where $x_0 = 0$, like $Q$ in the image above, that don’t arise in this manner. Lines that have the qualities of $Q$ can be though of as limits of lines coming from an unbounded sequence of points in $\aff^2$ (as displayed by in the image on the right above). These are to be interpreted as the points at infinity that we were seeking. To reiterate the image to the right above, as it’s the most insightful I believe, if we are considering a line $L$ through the origin of $k^3$, which doesn’t lie on the plane $x_0 = 0$, then the $L$ intersects only once with $(1, x_1, x_2) (=H_1)$, the hyperplane cut out by $x_0 = 1$. Now imagine that we are varying the line $L$ to begin to rotate towards the plane $x_0 = 0$ (as depicted in the second image by the line $Q$), so that we ultimately get it to lie in the plane $x_0 = 0$. Then the point of intersection with $H_1$ will then go off to infinity (look at the lines stretching farthest farthest to the right of in the second image) and disappears as the varying line $L$ comes to rest in the plane $x_0 = 0$. The limiting point, which determines the line $L$, is our point of intersection! The point at infinity!Definition 1. Let $k$ be a field. The Projective $n$-space over $k$, written $\pp^n (k)$, or just $\pp^n$ when context is clear, is defined to be the set of all lines through $(0, 0,\ldots, 0)$ in $k^{n+1}$. Moreover, any point $(x) = (x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n) 
eq (0, 0, \ldots, 0)$ determines a unique such that line that passes through the origin: the line is given by the set ${ \lambda x_0, \lambda x_1, \ldots, \lambda x_n \colon \lambda \in k }$. Two points $(x)$ and $(y)$ produce the same line if and only if there exists some nonzero $\lambda \in k$ such that $(x_0, \ldots, x_n) = (\lambda y_0, \ldots, \lambda y_n)$—if this is the case, we say that $(x)$ and $(y)$ are equivalent. Then $\pp^n (k)$ can be established by \[\pp^n (k) = (k^{n+1} \setminus (0, \ldots, 0) )/ \sim\]with the equivalence relation $(x_0, \ldots, x_n) \sim (y_0, \ldots, y_n)$ if and only if $(x_0, \ldots, x_n) = (\lambda y_0, \ldots, \lambda y_n)$ for some $\lambda \in k\setminus {0 }$. Equivalence classes of $(x_0, \ldots, x_n)$ is usually denoted by $(x_0 \colon \cdots \colon x_n) \in \pp^n (k)$. We call $x_0, \ldots, x_n$ the homogeneous or projective coordinates of the points $(x_0 \colon \cdots x_n )$.Example 1. The space $\pp^1 (r^2)$ is homeomorphic to $\mathbf{S}^1$, as shown below. As our initial construction of projective space went along, we can now say that we embed the affine space $\aff^n$ into $\pp^n (k)$ by the map $(x_0, \ldots, x_n ) \mapsto (1\colon x_1 \colon \cdots \colon x_n )$ whose image is defined to be the subset $U_0 = {(x_0 \colon \cdots \colon x_n ) \colon x_0 
eq 0 }$ of $\pp^n (k)$. In this way, by setting $x_0 = 1$, we can describe $ \aff^n$ as being a subset of $\pp^n (k)$. We call coordinates $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ the inhomogenous or affine coordinates on $U_0$. And the remaining points of $\pp^n (k)$ are of the form $(0 \colon x_1 \colon \cdots \colon x_n)$ (some authors identify this as being the set $H_{\infty} := { (x_0 \colon \cdots \colon x_n ) \in \pp^n (k) \colon x_0 = 0 }$). We “forget” the coordinate $x_0 = 0$ (as it’s zero) and $H_\infty$ is naturally bijective to $\pp^{n-1} (k)$—as it corresponds to the $1$-dimensional linear subspaces of $k^n$. Points in the set $H_\infty$ are said to be points at infinity, and so there is one such point in every direction in $k^n$. Additionally, $H_\infty$ is said to be the hyperplane at infinity.Example 1.  $\pp^1(k)$ can be decomposed into $\pp^1(k) = \aff^1 \cup \pp^0 (k)$. Now $\pp^0 (k)$ consists of a single point (easiest to think of $\pp^0$ as being all the lines which go through the origin of $k^1 = k$, which we identify as being the “point at infinity”, which we denote as ${ \infty } = \pp^0 (k)$. So then we write $\pp^1 (k) = \aff^1 \cup { \infty }$. Moreover, using a stereographic projection, we can understand $\pp^1 (k)$ as the set of lines through a fixed point:Recall that a line is described by $ax+by + c = 0$ where both $a, b$ are not zero. In looking at the projective plane, our attention is aimed at $(a \colon b)$. To emphasize $\pp^1$, if we consider $\pp^1 (r)$, we can notice that if $(x \colon y) \in \pp^1 (r)$ and $y 
eq 0$, then $(x, y) \sim (x/y, 1)$ as we $\lambda = 1/y$, and so the class $(x \colon y)$ contains a unique representative $(\ell, 1)$ where $\ell = x/y \in r$. So then we can think of $\pp^1 (r)$ as the following:\[\pp^1 (r) = \{ (x \colon 1 ) \colon x \in r \} \cup \{(1 \colon 0) \}\]where the points ${( x \colon 1 ) }$ form a real line and $(1 \colon 0 )$ is called the point at infinity.We can carry this on for greater dimensions of $\pp (r)$: If $x_0 
eq 0$, then $(x_0 , x_1, x_2) \sim (1,  rac{x_1}{x_0},  rac{x_2}{x_0} )$, and so \[\pp^2 (r) = \{ (1 \colon x_1 \colon x_2) \colon x_1,x_2 \in r \} \cup \{ (0 \colon a \colon b) \colon a,b \in r \}.\]And so the points of $ { (1 \colon x_1 \colon x_2) \colon x_1,y \in r }$ are in $1$-to-$1$ correspondence with the real plane, and the points in ${ (0 \colon a \colon b) \colon a,b \in r }$ are the points at infinity and can be identified with $\pp^1(r)$. Proposition 1. Parallel lines in the real plane intersect at a point at infinity. Proof. Let $L \colon y = mx + b$ and $L^\prime \colon y =mx +b^\prime$ define parallel lines that are unique in $r^2$. Now, if we take points $(k_1, k_2) \in r^2$, then this corresponds to lines $(k_1 \colon k_2) \in \pp^1 (r)$, and so, in analogy, lines in $r^2$ correspond to planes in $\pp^2 (r)$:  egin{align} L &= { (x \colon y \colon z) \colon mx-bz -y =0 }, \ L^\prime &= { ( x \colon y \colon z ) \colon mx-b^\prime z -y = 0 } .\end{align} Now as we want to find a common point we take the intersection of $L$ and $L^\prime$. A point of intersection $(x \colon y \colon z) \in L \cap L^\prime$ satisfies $mx+bz-y = mx+b^\prime -y $, which ultimately implies $z(b^\prime - b) =0$. If $b^\prime = b$, then this gives $L = L^\prime$ which contradicts the fact that they’re parallel; thus we must have $z=0$. Hence egin{align}L  \cap L^\prime &= { (x \colon y \colon 0 ) \colon mx-y=0 }  \ &= { (x \colon mx \colon 0) \colon x \in r } \ &= { (1 \colon m \colon 0) }, \end{align} and this is a point at infinity—a single point at infinity.Now as we have done before, we were able to describe $\pp^1(r) \simeq \mathbb S^1$, and the title of this post suggests, we can do the same for $\pp^1 (\cc)$, but this one is a bit cooler in my opinion.  If we let $k = \cc$, then we claim that $\pp^n$ is a compact space (with the classical topology, not Zariski). Let $$S^{2n+1} = {(a_0, \ldots, a_n) \in \cc^{n+1} \colon a_0 ^2 + \cdots + a_n ^2 = 1 }$$ be the unit sphere in $\cc^{n+1} = r^{2n+2}$. So this space is compact as it’s closed and bounded. Now there is a surjective map \(S^{2n+1} 	o \pp^n (\cc), (a_0, \ldots, a_n) \longmapsto (a_0 \colon \cdots \colon a_n)\)which is clearly surjective, and so images of compact sets are compact under continuous maps, then $\pp^n (k)$ is also compact. By what we established in Example 1, $\pp^1 (\cc) = \cc^1 \cup {\infty }$. So it is topologically a sphere, and we can visualize it in $r^3,$ and it is indeed the Riemann sphere $\widehat{\cc}$Ultimately we hope to do algebraic geometry over this new geometry we’ve just constructed, and in fact, most algebraic geometry is done over projective spaces, so we’ll proceed in the rest of this post establishing how can start to do it.Let $A = k[x_0, \ldots , x_n]$. If we take $f \in A$ and $P = (a_0 \colon \cdots \colon a_n) \in \pp^n$, then $f(P)$ doesn’t make sense. For example, $f(x_0, x_1) =x^2_1 - x_0$ then $f(1, 1) =0$ but $f(-1, -1) 
eq 0$, even though $(1 \colon 1)$ and $(-1 \colon -1)$ are the same point in $\pp^n$. We need to adapt your approach, and we do this by requiring a polynomial $f \in A$ of degree $d$ to be homogeneous, that is, \(f(\lambda x_0, \ldots, \lambda x_n) = \lambda^d f(x_0, \ldots, x_n) 	ext{ for all $\lambda \in k^\ast$},\) which implies that $(\lambda x_0, \ldots, \lambda x_n) = 0 $ if and only if $f(x_0, \ldots, x_n) =0$, and so this makes sense when $f(P) = 0$ or $f(P) 
eq 0$. Definition 2. Let $T \subseteq A$ be a set of homogeneous polynomials. Let \(V(T) = \{ P \in \pp^n \colon f(P) = 0 	ext{ for all $f \in T$} \}.\)For a homogeneous polynomial $f \in A$, we define $D(f) := \pp^n \setminus V(f)$. As we should expect, the definitions on $V$ give rise to the Zariski topology on $\pp^n$. Definition 3. A projective algebraic variety is a subset of $\pp^n$ of the form $V(T)$, together with the induced Zariski topology. To be clear, we have the following Zariski topology on (algebraic) projective varieties:Lemma 1. Projective Varieties satisfy the folowing axioms of the closed sets of a topology on $\pp^n_k$; that tis, we have the following:The union of finitely many projective varieties is a projective variety.The intersection of any number of projective varieties is a projective varietyThe empty set and $\pp^n_k$ are projective varieties. To move further down the line in, we have to define further objects.Definition 4. A graded ring is a ring $S$ together with a decomposition into abelian groups \(S =  igoplus_{d\geq 0} S_d\) such thatfor all $d 
eq e$, we have $S_d \cap S_e = { 0 },$ and$S_d \cdot S_e \subset S_{d+e}$.The elements of $S_d$ are called the homogeneous elements of degree $d$.Example 2. An important example that we’ll use is that we can define \(k^d [x_0, \ldots, x_n] := \{ f \in k[x_0, \ldots, x_n] \colon f 	ext{ is homogeneous of degree $d$} \} \cup \{0 \}\) to grade $k[x_0, \ldots, x_n]$ in the following way:\(k[x_0, \ldots, x_n] =  igoplus_{d \geq 0} k^d [x_0, \ldots, x_n ].\) So, an element $f \in S_d$ is homogeneous of degree d. Furthermore, an ideal $J$ in $S$ is called homogeneous if it is generated by homogeneous elements; that is,\[J =  igoplus_{d \geq 0} (J \cap S_d).\]so an ideal $J$ is homogeneous if and only if every element $f \in J$ has a unique composition $f = f_0 +\cdots +f_N,$ where each $f_i \in J$ is a homogeneous of degree $d_i$. And a homogeneous ideal $I$ is called prime if and only if for any pair of homgeneous elemements $f, g$ in a graded ring $S$, we have $fg \in I$ if and only $f \in I$ or $g \in I$. Now, as I have just thrown so many definitions at you, what actually are we doing right now? What we’re doing is essentially trying to build up to an analogous construction of $\spec (R)$, for some ring $R$, of affine schemes in projective space.
